journal_entry.rationale: Inventory loss due to natural disaster is recognized as an expense (loss). The debit to Loss on Inventory Destruction (or similar expense account) records the loss, while the credit to Inventory reduces the asset. Under IFRS, casualty losses are recognized in profit or loss when the loss occurs. The $2,000 represents the carrying amount of the destroyed inventory. No tax implications as this is a loss event, not a taxable supply or purchase.

output.reason: Inventory destroyed = asset decrease. The inventory account must be credited to remove the destroyed goods from the books. The loss will be debited to an expense or loss account.

credit_classifier.reason: Inventory destroyed = asset decrease. The inventory account must be credited to remove the destroyed goods from the books. The loss will be debited to an expense or loss account.

output.reason: Inventory destroyed by typhoon is an abnormal loss/casualty. The debit records the expense increase (loss on inventory destruction). The inventory asset decrease is the credit side, not classified here.

output.reason: Entry correctly records casualty loss from natural disaster. Debit to Loss on Inventory Destruction (expense increase) of $2,000 and credit to Inventory (asset decrease) of $2,000. Balance verified (debits = credits). Amount matches transaction text. Casualty losses are not taxable events, so no tax lines required. Directionality correct: expense increases with debit, asset decreases with credit. IFRS requires casualty losses to be recognized in profit or loss when incurred.
